claim: Claude Code 4.5+ can one-shot MVP creation for projects and reliably build things users can trust, marking a capability threshold where AI-assisted coding becomes genuinely useful for production work
actionable: Start using Claude Code 4.5+ for MVP-stage projects requiring integrated workflows (UI+backend), testing it on simple dashboards or financial models before moving to complex systems
source_title: Claude Code for Finance + The Global Memory Shortage: Doug O'Laughlin, SemiAnalysis

claim: Claude Code adoption is tracking exponentially at ~4% of GitHub commits within 2 weeks (as of Dec 2024), suggesting it will reach 25-50% of all code generation by end of 2025
actionable: Track Claude Code adoption rates monthly using GitHub commit API queries and bigquery to monitor exponential growth curves and adjust productivity tooling decisions accordingly
source_title: Claude Code for Finance + The Global Memory Shortage: Doug O'Laughlin, SemiAnalysis

claim: Using Claude Code systematically for information work (financial analysis, portfolio grading, data synthesis) requires treating it like a junior analyst—you must review outputs for hygiene, not just accept them as-is, since it makes mistakes consistently but they're fixable at the margin
actionable: For each Claude Code task, define a rubric upfront, have the model grade its own work against that rubric in a separate context window (not embedded in the initial prompt) to avoid context bias, then do a fresh review pass yourself
source_title: Claude Code for Finance + The Global Memory Shortage: Doug O'Laughlin, SemiAnalysis

claim: Context rot is a real problem in long multi-turn Claude Code sessions; separate task execution from rubric evaluation into different context windows to preserve freshness and avoid co-mingling information that biases the model
actionable: When building extended Claude Code workflows, run task and evaluation in separate invocations with distinct context windows; avoid putting the rubric in the same prompt as the task setup
source_title: Claude Code for Finance + The Global Memory Shortage: Doug O'Laughlin, SemiAnalysis

claim: The 1M context window (vs 200K) is a major UX breakthrough because your project takes up less of the window, giving you more 'oomph' for work; multi-agent subagents with their own context windows compound this advantage by not wasting the main context on inter-agent communication
actionable: Use 1M context windows for single-cohesive projects (not multi-part tasks split across sessions); if using multi-agent flows, design subagents to work independently and hand off work via structured outputs, not via passing large intermediate states in the main context
source_title: Claude Code for Finance + The Global Memory Shortage: Doug O'Laughlin, SemiAnalysis

claim: Claude Code Opus 4.6 generalizes across information work (research, data analysis, code) better than specialized models like Code Interpreter, whereas Code Interpreter (e.g., o1) is narrowly optimized for coding via RL and fails at non-coding tasks
actionable: For multi-modal information work (coding + research + analysis), use Opus 4.6; for pure coding benchmarks, Code Interpreter may score higher but don't use it for mixed tasks like 'generate code AND analyze data AND write a report'
source_title: Claude Code for Finance + The Global Memory Shortage: Doug O'Laughlin, SemiAnalysis

claim: Sub-agents are significantly more reliable than agent-teams in Claude Code; agent-teams are experimental and perform worse because they lack reinforcement learning to understand when to delegate vs. execute
actionable: Use sub-agents for Claude Code workflows where each sub-agent has a clear, focused task and returns structured results to the main agent; avoid agent-teams for now pending further RL optimization
source_title: Claude Code for Finance + The Global Memory Shortage: Doug O'Laughlin, SemiAnalysis

claim: Separating rubric evaluation from task execution in Claude Code prevents the model from co-mingling information and becoming biased; a fresh context window for review behaves like a new perspective, catching errors the task-execution context missed
actionable: Design your Claude Code prompts in two phases: (1) task phase with clear goal, (2) separate evaluation phase using a rubric; avoid combining them to get more objective reviews
source_title: Claude Code for Finance + The Global Memory Shortage: Doug O'Laughlin, SemiAnalysis

claim: AI models are now better than human industry experts on GDP-val (applied white-collar work benchmarks), scoring 70% vs 50% (industry expert parity); this marks a practical AGI threshold for knowledge work automation
actionable: Use GDP-val as a benchmark to track when AI tooling exceeds your own expertise; when a model scores above 65-70%, treat it as a strong candidate for automating routine aspects of that knowledge work
source_title: Claude Code for Finance + The Global Memory Shortage: Doug O'Laughlin, SemiAnalysis

claim: Excel and Bloomberg as IDEs for analysis work are functionally dead; agents generating charts in matplotlib or data in code are faster and higher-quality than manual spreadsheet manipulation, so don't rebuild legacy workflows
actionable: Stop building new analysis workflows in Excel; design all new financial/data work as code-first (Python+matplotlib or similar), letting Claude Code generate charts and tables directly, then consume the output as images or data exports
source_title: Claude Code for Finance + The Global Memory Shortage: Doug O'Laughlin, SemiAnalysis

claim: DRAM and HBM memory prices will increase 100%+ in next 2 years due to AI demand pulling capacity away from traditional DRAM into HBM, combined with underinvestment in fab cleanroom capacity during the 2020-2024 shortage cycle
actionable: If planning infrastructure buildouts requiring high memory density (AI training/inference), lock in DRAM/HBM supply contracts now; if in consumer market, expect phone/device prices to rise due to memory cost pass-through by 2026
source_title: Claude Code for Finance + The Global Memory Shortage: Doug O'Laughlin, SemiAnalysis

claim: HBM-to-DRAM conversion ratio is 4:1 (losing 3 capacity units to convert 1 unit to HBM), creating a supply squeeze; KB cache offload to DDR4 is now viable because of this shortage, resurrecting CXL memory expansion tech as a workaround
actionable: In infrastructure planning, account for the 4:1 HBM conversion cost when budgeting memory; explore CXL memory expansion boards to attach legacy DDR4 when HBM supply is constrained
source_title: Claude Code for Finance + The Global Memory Shortage: Doug O'Laughlin, SemiAnalysis

claim: Million-token context windows will remain capped for 5-10 years due to memory bottlenecks; 100M+ context is not practically achievable; 'context rationing' via token budgets or recursive LMs will be the workaround, not expanded window sizes
actionable: Design long-context workflows assuming 1M-token ceiling; use recursive language models or multi-turn context stratification instead of expecting single-turn 100M-token windows; plan for 'context budgeting' frameworks for agentic systems
source_title: Claude Code for Finance + The Global Memory Shortage: Doug O'Laughlin, SemiAnalysis

claim: For writing, sleep after outlining and wake up with a fresh context window to write; this beats writing in the same session as ideation because you get 60-75% of the piece immediately and fill the rest cleanly
actionable: When writing, do ideation and outlining one day, sleep, then open a fresh document the next morning and write in one pass; you'll get higher-quality output with fewer iterations than staying in the same context
source_title: Claude Code for Finance + The Global Memory Shortage: Doug O'Laughlin, SemiAnalysis

claim: Use Claude Code for ideation and outlining (not final writing); let it brainstorm and structure, but you write the final prose because LM-generated prose lacks voice and the writer's understanding
actionable: In Claude Code workflows for content: use it to generate outlines, bullet points, and structure; write the final prose yourself based on that scaffold to ensure voice and accuracy
source_title: Claude Code for Finance + The Global Memory Shortage: Doug O'Laughlin, SemiAnalysis
